title.emphasis: Section 4: A Strategic Approach to Intellectual Property

section.para: The management of intellectual property (IP) is a critical component of any artistic commission. The default rules under U.S. copyright law, combined with the specific hazards of California's labor laws, necessitate a deliberate and strategic approach. This section outlines three models for handling IP, recommending one as the standard for its legal soundness and mutual protection, and issuing a strong warning against a commonly used but legally perilous alternative.

title.emphasis: Subsection 4.1: The Default Rule and the Limited License (Model 1)

section.para: Under the U.S. Copyright Act, the creator of an original work is the initial owner of the copyright from the moment the work is fixed in a tangible medium.33 The act of commissioning a work does not automatically transfer copyright ownership to the patron. In the absence of a written agreement stating otherwise, the artist retains full copyright to the commissioned piece.35

The simplest IP model, therefore, is to adhere to this default and formalize it with a Limited License. In this scenario, the artist retains full copyright ownership and grants the patron a narrow, non-exclusive license for a specific, defined use. For example, the SOW could grant the patron a license "for personal, non-commercial display within a private residence only".3 This model is ideal for initial, low-stakes "probe" commissions where the patron wishes to assess an artist's talent with minimal financial and legal commitment.38

title.emphasis: Subsection 4.2: The Option-to-Purchase Agreement (Model 2 - Recommended Standard)

section.para: For commissions where the patron's goal is to acquire full ownership of both the physical artwork and its underlying copyright, the Option-to-Purchase Agreement is the most legally sophisticated and recommended model.3 This structure, adapted from best practices in the film and entertainment industries, provides a secure and enforceable mechanism for transferring rights to an asset that does not yet exist.40
The agreement is deconstructed into two parts within a single SOW 40:

listitem.para: The Option: The patron makes an initial, non-refundable payment known as the Option Fee. This fee is not a deposit on the artwork itself; it is the legal consideration paid in exchange for securing an exclusive and irrevocable right—the option—to purchase the full copyright of the completed artwork at a pre-agreed price.40 This fee typically represents 25-50% of the total project cost. The SOW defines the Option Period, which is the timeframe during which the artist will create the work and the patron holds this exclusive right.3

The Purchase: The pre-agreed total cost for the artwork and its copyright is defined as the Purchase Price. The patron exercises the option by formally accepting the delivered artwork and paying the remaining balance of the Purchase Price (i.e., Purchase Price minus the Option Fee). This action triggers the "purchase" part of the agreement, and a pre-written copyright assignment clause within the SOW becomes effective at that moment.40

section.para: This structure is legally superior because the initial contract is for a present, enforceable right (the option), not for a future, non-existent asset (the copyright). It also provides mutual protection:

listitem.para: Patron Protection: If the final artwork is unsatisfactory, the patron can decline to exercise the option and is not obligated to make the final payment. Their only loss is the Option Fee.3

Artist Protection: If the patron declines the work, the artist retains the non-refundable Option Fee as compensation for their labor and materials up to that point. This functions as a built-in "kill fee".42 The artist also retains the artwork and all rights to it, free to sell it elsewhere.

title.emphasis: Subsection 4.3: The Work-for-Hire Trap (Model 3 - A Cautionary Analysis)

section.para: A "Work Made for Hire" (WFH) agreement is a common but profoundly misunderstood and misused tool in creative commissions. Under federal copyright law, a work can only be a WFH under two very specific conditions: (1) it is created by an employee within the scope of their employment, or (2) it is a specially commissioned work that falls into one of nine narrow statutory categories and is accompanied by a written agreement signed by both parties stating it is a WFH.36

The nine categories are: a contribution to a collective work, part of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, a translation, a supplementary work, a compilation, an instructional text, a test, answer material for a test, or an atlas.36 A typical commissioned painting, drawing, or sculpture

does not fit into any of these categories. Consequently, a WFH clause in a contract for such a work is legally ineffective under federal law for its intended purpose of transferring copyright. The correct legal mechanism is a copyright assignment, as utilized in the Option-to-Purchase model.

More dangerously, in California, using a WFH agreement with an individual creator triggers a specific and perilous legal trap. California Unemployment Insurance Code §686 and Labor Code §3351.5(c) create a legal presumption that any individual who signs a WFH agreement is a statutory employee for purposes of workers' compensation and unemployment insurance.47 This means that by using a WFH contract, a patron inadvertently reclassifies the artist as an employee, triggering all the attendant legal obligations and liabilities that this entire model is designed to avoid. Using a WFH agreement with an individual artist in California is not only legally incorrect for copyright purposes but is also a direct path to worker misclassification.

title.emphasis: Subsection 4.4: Moral Rights and the Optional Waiver

section.para: Separate from the economic rights of copyright are an artist's Moral Rights, which protect the artist's personal connection to their work. These are primarily established in California by the California Art Preservation Act (CAPA) and at the federal level by the Visual Artists Rights Act (VARA).49 They consist of:

listitem.para: The Right of Attribution: The right to be identified as the creator of the work, or to disclaim authorship if the work is distorted.
The Right of Integrity: The right to prevent any intentional defacement, mutilation, alteration, or destruction of the work.

section.para: These rights are retained by the artist even after the physical artwork is sold and the copyright is assigned.52 However, unlike in many other countries, moral rights in the United States can be waived through an express written instrument signed by the artist.54 For patrons who require absolute control over the physical artwork—for example, if it is to be integrated into a building where it might one day need to be altered or removed—it is advisable to include a clear, explicit, and separate
Waiver of Moral Rights clause in the SOW.

row.entry: Low. Cannot commercially exploit the work.
Low. Retains full control of IP for future licensing.
Artist initially, transfers to Patron upon final payment.
Acquiring full commercial rights to a new, custom artwork.
Low. Can reject final work, losing only the option fee.
Low. Guaranteed compensation (kill fee) if work is rejected.
**Minimal.** This is the legally correct and safest method.
Legally remains with Artist (for fine art); Patron *thinks* they own it.
High. Ineffective for copyright transfer and creates employment liability.
High. Ambiguous rights and potential for disputes.
**Extreme.** Triggers a statutory presumption of employment.
